speaker: interviewer
text: March 9, 1997. The survivor is Albert Roland. Interviewer is [INAUDIBLE]. We're interviewing in Denver, Colorado, United States of America. And it's being done in English. When we ended the last tape, you were talking about what happened in-- after the war broke out. Let me go back and ask you, when you left with your two brothers-- you said September 6-- how long were you gone from when you left and then you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were gone a couple days. And when we came back, well, soon enough as I mentioned before, we had to wear yellow bands. And the scenery that we saw, those three people hanging on the gallows in the marketplace, was a very harsh disappointment to us. So we had to make a decision on how to get out of the situation because we know that this situation is not going to be like it is now, that every day is going to be worse and worse and worse, which it was, against Jews. And we didn't have any cooperation of the Gentile people. There were not cooperating with us against the Germans. They thought the Germans were going to bring them a lot of good things, which they were later on disappointed too. So to take off that band, that yellow band, that meant like a death sentence, because by decree-- by decree they could shoot you on the spot. Because it was not a law to wear the yellow band. It was by decree. And this is-- there's no questions asked. But we had to make a decision to take it off and try to-- to get farther away from the Germans in about 60, 70 kilometers from our city because our city was annexed to Germany so it was not anymore Poland. It was already Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me get it-- let me--

speaker: interviewee
text: And 60 miles from there, it was the German Protectorate, they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what month was that? You said the Germans invaded Lodz in September 6, then you left and went to Warsaw. And then you said there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: They invaded not September 6. September 6 we kept running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what I meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in a day or two later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you go to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Warsaw? We went-- that was the 11 of November, you know, when we saw those three people on the gallows hanging. So we left a few days later. We left. Into-- you couldn't just go with anything, but you have to go to the railroad station, buy a ticket, which to expose yourself, you know, as a Jew without the yellow band which was a decree, they could just shoot you, or do anything they wanted, you know? But we made the decision to-- to go, my brother and I. We went to the railroad station, laid very low, you know, not to expose ourselves too much. We got two tickets. And then we got on the train. We had to wait, you know, until the train arrived. We got on the train. And there was the same thing, you know, because the Germans. And they were not only the Germans from Germany but Lodz itself had a lot of Volksdeutsche that were Germans that lived in Poland. They lived in Lodz because of Lodz was a textile-- a lot of textile industry. And this is-- they were masters in the textile industry and the textile trade. So, but beside that, we decided, you know, we got the ticket. We got on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take your yellow armband off?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't get a ticket without taking the yellow band off. You see? But we took the yellow band off like nothing else. And we had to be brave about it. You get the tickets, get on the train. Once the train started moving, we knew we were halfway-- halfway there. Good enough, we came to that station-- 60, 70 kilometers from Lodz. And over there we didn't have to have a band. That was one thing that we-- we got rid of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother stayed in-- in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other brother, Aaron, did he-- had he ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had never come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He just went with his friend. We didn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother decided not to go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was not well. So she said, because the German-- the radio said that the war is going to two to three months, you know, and then it's going to be over. So my mother said, you kids go. You know, I cannot go with you. I have to have my home, my bed. But after two, three months, things were over, so you come back home, and everything is going to be fine again, you know, wishful thinking from a parent. But as we came farther, we saw more bad things, what the Germans did, you know, coming to Warsaw with the train. You know, all the bridges on the-- on the river Wisla were bumped out. Only one bridge was left, you know. That was the only way we could go on the other side, which we have to go east. We had to go from west railroad station to the eastern railroad station in order to get closer to the Russians. That was our aim, because we thought if we can make it to the Russians, you know, at least we are not going to be-- we're going to be safe, you know, from anybody taking our lives. So when we came to the eastern railroad station, you know, again, we had to wait for the train. We had to go on the station, you know. There were a lot of SS, you know, and all kinds of German militaries, you know. And it wasn't safe. But it was already in the evening, you know. We could hide our faces. And good enough, we were able to wait until the-- the next train is coming. And we got on the train. So getting on the train, you know, we were going in the right direction, going east. And there was a river-- Bug. And River Bug, we had to cross the River Bug. But there was a little town where you didn't have to cross the River Bug. Somehow, I don't remember how it was that you could get around it. So that was our destination, which the train was going there. So once we arrived at the station, you know, the name of the city was Malkin. And it was about 10 or 11 o'clock in the evening. So the Germans started shooting as soon as the train stopped, you know. They started shooting. We saw fire from all sides. Then the Germans started hollering to disembark the train. And right away, as soon as we got out of the train, they said, Jews to the right and Poles to the left. So anyway, we tried to-- not to go to the right. We tried to go to the left, but they took those lanterns, those railroaders, you know, were using, you know, at night. And they were shining in everybody's faces. And if they recognized a Jew, they beat them up and they had German shepherds with them. You know, they were tearing them to pieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a tragedy. They recognized my brother because he was light complected. And he was running already to-- to the right, you know. And I was going-- you know, I didn't want to be left alone because I wanted to be with my brother. The Jews were to the right you say? So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the left first, you know. But then, when they recognized my brother, they started beating on him so he was running, and the dogs after him. I was running in his same direction, and we finally joined up and we went where all the Jewish people were. And they took us to-- to a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] police command, [NON-ENGLISH], they call it. And over there they gathered us and they started searching. Because some people, if they have some watches or gold rings or, you know, whatever, then they started beating us up. Maybe it was a good thing that they were beating us because I got such a bad taste for the Germans that I-- I wanted to be on the-- on the other end of the world, you know, as far as I could be from the Germans. And that-- that was our idea, just to be as far as we can, not to be too close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any valuables with you? Did you have any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had only 100 zlotys, which was like $100, which was not much for the two of us. But at least, ans my brother had it hidden, you know, in the shoe. And he was scared, you know, they'll found it. He was going to get a, you know, a roughing up, or they could shoot him too, you know? But they didn't find it. Ans we were lucky. And we went to the no-man's land. You know, there was demarcation line which was a temporary border between the Russians and the Germans, which was done between, you know, between an agreement between-- between the Russians and the Germans, because the Russians took Eastern Poland which was Western Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, when we came into no-man's land, you know, it started snowing, and people laying on street. The Russians didn't let us in, didn't let go farther. So we got stuck over there. You didn't have papers or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't need papers. It was-- who had? Nobody knew, you know, where it's going to be best to stay or to go, or to run or to walk, you know? It was such a situation, a mish-mosh. So when we came on that no-man's land, you know, people with families, with little children, you know, and the wind was blowing, the cold wind with that wet snow. It was a horrible thing. And here's the night, you know, falling. Anyway, we had to stay about three nights in that no-man's land.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay, in a shelter or outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside. There were no shelters. There were no shelter. If there was some place, a little shack, you know, people were stuffing in and the shack fell apart, you know, because everybody wanted to be safe from the-- from the wet weather. But anyway, after the third day, you know, started to talk to the-- to the Russians, to the [NON-ENGLISH] they called them. The-- they were special, special military for the-- for the border-- border military. And finally, it took a few brave people, you know. It was a gate, you know. We pried open the gate and we started going, even though they didn't let us, we started going. So they came with cavalry, you know, on horses. And they started to push us back, but nobody could push us back. The will to get away was so strong, you know. But at least behind us, you know, they were able to cut off-- I don't know. I didn't look back. They were cutting off certain people. The ones that went across, you know, they let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many do you think got through?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hundreds? Thousands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was thinking about themselves. Who was thinking about somebody-- as long as I had my brother next to me and he felt that he's got me next to him, you know, that's all we wanted to be. And then we have to walk about 18 kilometers to a station, to a railroad station. And that railroad station came only once-- once a day. If you don't catch the railroad station, you have to wait another 24 hours for the next one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in the Russians zone at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [INAUDIBLE], where the Russians were, yeah. And there were a lot of people. The train couldn't take in possibly all the people. But the train came into the station.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were lucky to get in. You know, there was a little-- you know, there was some steps outside the wagon, you know, where they had the brakes, you know, on top. You know, we went into that little thing, and about 10 people got stuck-- stuffed in there. Everybody wanted to get away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And outside the train, people like flies, you know, were sticking to the train, and on the top and on the sides.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they sell tickets, or you just hung on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the train went and everybody got on.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train went-- the train went through a-- a tunnel, you know. And a lot of people got brushed off the train. You know, we heard screams, you know. But it was such a time that screams didn't mean much, you know. Until we got to the next station, what was-- what was-- the next station was Lapy. We went in direction of Bialystok. And the next station was the same thing, you know. But you didn't have already that many people. So it was easier-- easier to get on the train. We had to pay for the fare. And finally we got to-- to Bialystok, Bialystok was a lot of refugees, lot of refugees. There was no place but to sleep, but to-- nothing. The schools were full. The hospitals were full. Wherever there was a public building, you know, and private people, some of them took in some, and some people were afraid because they didn't know the people. They were afraid for something will happen to them or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally we got a place, my brother and I, were to be. And we had heard, you know, that we could go to work, you know, work for the Russians. So I went to-- to work for the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- by hand, you know, those parquet floors-- you know, I was in the offices. You know, you have to finish to parquet floors. Now they have machinery. We had to do it by hand. And I worked a few days. Then we decided to go from Bialystok to Lutsk. I had my cousin was in military. And he was able to run away, you know, and he settled somehow with a family over there. And we were able to get the address where he was at. So we went to Lutsk. Good enough, there was a nice family, very friendly. They received us very warmly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, yeah. And it was-- it was really some-- a little warm, you know, when everything was so rough and-- and horrible. But this was not the end of our journey. We know we are still too close to the Germans. And we knew we had a feeling that the Germans are not going to stop there. They're going to go after the Russians, you know, which-- which later on they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we wanted to go to Russia because that was the only way to get farther away. So we had to register to go to work to Russia. You couldn't go because the old border was still closed. You couldn't penetrate the Russian border. So we had to register. And after a week or so-- or so, they took us on, what they call it, freight-- a freight train, not a passenger train, on freight trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard. It was dirty. But finally, we could make our way farther away from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you had to register to work, did you have to take Russian citizenship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you-- you just signed up to work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just if you want to go to work, yeah. And they had a man, which was every wagon, every car. I said, wait, every car had one of the Russian-- he was probably from the KGB or something, you know. And he was the one to oversee these people, you know. Because, you know, the communists were always afraid for-- for not to have any enemies, you know, penetrating, you know, into Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we were very friendly with him. He was very friendly with us, you know. And we finally went through the Russian border. It was Christmas night, 1940-- 19-- no, 1939. In New Year's, 1940, yeah, Christmas night. And we went about 18 days. And you know how these-- these-- they're not scheduled. These trains are not scheduled. So we used to stay sometimes for a day or two on the-- on the side tracks. You know, they were pushing back and pushing us forward. Did you have beds on the train? You said they were freight cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, straw spread out on the floor. This is how we were sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got so many-- so much lice, we got-- it was horrible. I didn't believe it. They were crawling all over us. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- and how did they feed you? What did-- what did they do to feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. They didn't feed us on every station, you know. We had to go out and buy whatever somebody could.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still had money?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't-- you didn't need too much to buy a piece of bread. Bread was cheap in Russia. If you wanted milk, if you wanted something else, was expensive. But bread was very cheap.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is how we made our way through. When we came already on the-- the place of destination, then they started feeding us. Then they took us off the train. And they had quarters for us. You know, for single people, they had four or six people in a-- in a room, six beds, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a-- they call it the stolovaya, which is like a restaurant. But you couldn't-- there was no menu. Whatever they cooked, you know, they fed you, you had to be fed with.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get cleaned up or get rid the lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to [NON-ENGLISH], which is a bath house. And the lice you couldn't get rid of because, first of all, they had steam chambers, you know, to steam the-- the cloth against lice. The heat will kill them. But people don't have enough-- enough wood or coal at the houses to heat up their homes, so they steal everything, you know, to take it home. So they had-- they had barely warm water, you know, not hot water to take a shower. And the steam-- the steam room-- those lice got more lively because it wasn't hot. It was just a little bit warm. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we put on our clothes, it was terrible, worse than before. But anyway, it was OK. You know, nobody was shooting at you. Nobody was after you life, you know. So you're young, you know. It's easier to get adopted, you know, to the circumstances, to the surrounding. And finally, we went to work. Was your brother still-- were you still with you brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. The other brother, we didn't know where he is. Later we found out. And so we-- we worked at-- they sent me to school and to become an electrician because I didn't have no trade on a factory. We had to go and work on a factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they decided they're going to-- since I was the youngest of the group, so I went to school. And I went three years to school. And finally I had to make good grades, which I did, in order to make more money when I go to work. So you just went to school, or did you work also?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. You went to-- for practical learning, you know. They teach you theory in school, then you go to the factory for practical work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they trade--

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave you, paid you, which wasn't much, 150 rubles a month, which was not much, very little-- very little. It was not easy because the bread that they were selling, you know, was awful. We were not used to it. And I was always, as far as food is concerned, even back home-- I was always, you know, picky. You know, if it didn't taste good, I wouldn't eat. And I was skinny because of that. The bread was so sour, like vinegar. So that's all I ate was bread, because I couldn't afford for the stipend to buy anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, time went by, and finally I made the exams, you know. And I passed from one grade to the next one. I had the high grade. Then I went to work. What we think training you to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electrician. Yeah. And I became motor-- what they call the-- the wires in the motor-- armature winding. Which it was-- it was more paid than the, you know, electrical in the buildings. You know, here is different. Here building trade pays more, but over there, it was less. So you had to adapt yourself to the circumstances. And I was working and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Lutsk, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lutsk, is that where you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Lutsk-- Lutsk was in the occupied zone from the-- that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you end--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Shumerlia. That was Shumerlia. That was Chuvash Autonomous Republic. The Chuvash people are very poor people, not educated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but you live in your own-- you know, with your own people, your own surrounding. And we didn't know the language so we couldn't mix with other people. But little by little, if you have to go to work, you have to learn how to speak and so forth. Especially in the school, it was even harder because you have to make homework. And so I learned everything by heart. I learned how to read. But it's easier to learn how to read than to speak. So I learned the lesson by heart. And next day, if I had to answer, if they called me to the blackboard, so I-- I did a good job because I knew it by heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language were you taught in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian, yeah. Russian language is everything is different. And you know, even the ABCs, the writing is different. But you'll learn, you'll learn. So when I started working, I was a good worker. And this is how it makes it easier life if you devote yourself and people see that you do a-- that you do a good job. They'll like you. And they will try to help too. The boss from on the job, you know, was very happy with me. And it made it easier on me, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time then, Albert?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was by about 20-- 20 or 21. '39, 21. 21 I was, 21 going on 22. Yeah. And then until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they started the war in '41 with the Russians and the Germans, so then it started to be very bad with food because the Russians said that we have to feed the army first, you know, which they had to, and they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the civilian population was very-- had to struggle. There was nothing to buy, nothing to even-- and those restaurants from the-- from the factory restaurants, you know, it was very little food. And the food got worst. The quality of food was got worse and worse. So then, if they gave you cabbage soup, which is a favorite of the Russians, that cabbage [INAUDIBLE], so you get two pieces of cabbage and then all water. And the cabbage was a rotten cabbage with worms. And you had to eat it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to take the worms out, those little white worms, you know. And you had to take it out and-- and sip the water, so-- to stay alive. But we saw that's getting not good, you know. So we were thinking, my brother and I, how to go a little farther from the Germans because it looks like the Germans-- we read in the paper that the Germans getting closer and closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop this tape and start another one in a minute.
